name: Inventory Limit
description: Inventory Limit is a pressure mechanic that asks the player to manage bodily, environmental, or resource constraints over time.
design_notes: Inventory Limit should be designed around a clear player promise, a readable trigger, and a predictable result. For survival mechanics, the important design work is deciding when the mechanic is allowed, how much commitment it creates, what feedback confirms success, and what counter-pressure keeps it from solving every situation. Strong tuning makes the mechanic feel intentional even when it is used repeatedly or combined with adjacent systems.
design_purpose: Give the player a readable way to use inventory limit as part of the core loop.
Create meaningful choices around timing, cost, risk, positioning, or long-term planning.
Connect inventory limit to adjacent systems so it supports the larger game design instead of acting as an isolated feature.
player_fantasy: The player feels vulnerable but resourceful under ongoing pressure.

parameters.name: max_value
parameters.description: Maximum capacity of the survival resource.

parameters.name: decay_rate
parameters.description: How quickly the resource changes without player action.

parameters.name: critical_threshold
parameters.description: Point at which warnings or penalties begin.

edge_cases: Inventory Limit is triggered during a scene transition, pause, respawn, or save/load boundary.
Inventory Limit overlaps with another high-priority state such as damage, stun, dialogue, menu focus, or cutscene control.
Inventory Limit runs at extreme frame rates, high latency, very large coordinates, or after long play sessions.
common_bugs: Inventory Limit state does not reset correctly after interruption or reload.
Feedback for Inventory Limit plays even when the mechanic fails or is rejected.
Inventory Limit produces different results between frame rates, platforms, or network clients.
balancing_notes: Make Inventory Limit strong enough to justify attention but limited enough that other mechanics keep their purpose.
Tune costs, cooldowns, ranges, and feedback together; changing only one number can shift the mechanic from optional to dominant.
accessibility_notes: Provide clear visual, audio, or haptic feedback when Inventory Limit becomes available, succeeds, or fails.
Avoid relying on a single sensory cue or extremely narrow timing unless the game offers assists or alternate settings.

implementation_notes.unity: Implement Inventory Limit with engine-agnostic state first, then bind it to MonoBehaviour, CharacterController, Rigidbody, UI Toolkit, or Netcode systems as appropriate.
implementation_notes.godot: Model Inventory Limit as a node or resource-driven state and update deterministic pieces from _physics_process when timing or collision matters.
implementation_notes.unreal: Implement Inventory Limit with Actor Components, CharacterMovement, Gameplay Ability System, UMG, or replicated gameplay state depending on the mechanic scope.
implementation_notes.web: Implement Inventory Limit as data-owned state updated from the main loop; keep rendering, input, and simulation boundaries explicit for Canvas, Phaser, Three.js, or custom JavaScript.
